next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The lamb was overcooked.

Hermione noticed this the way she noticed most things about Malfoy — sideways, while thinking about something else. He'd been in the kitchen for forty minutes, which was longer than usual, and when he set the plates down in the library she saw the edges of the chops gone grey and tight. He didn't mention it. She didn't mention it. They ate at the long table between stacks of Abelard Malfoy's correspondence and a genealogical chart she'd been cross-referencing against anchoring point locations, and the lamb was overcooked and fine and she ate all of it.

Three weeks. She'd been in the Manor for three weeks and she was eating overcooked lamb in a library that rearranged its own shelves and not finding this remarkable.

He was reading the Abelard letters while he ate, one hand holding a chop and the other turning pages. He'd switched to the originals after she'd found a transcription error in the copies — a single diacritical mark that changed "to anchor" to "to sever," which was the kind of mistake that could have sent them down the wrong research path for days. He'd been quietly furious about it. Not at her. At the transcription, at whoever had made the copies three hundred years ago, at the accumulated carelessness of his own family's record-keeping. She'd watched him go through the first twenty letters checking every mark against the originals, his handwriting in the margins getting smaller and more precise as he went, and she'd felt something that was not professional satisfaction.

Now he was eating with one hand and annotating with...
open_hooks_to_carry: The Mark memory flood: specific, sensory — Voldemort's study, sixteen years old, his father's hand, the pain that made him pass out. Hermione feels the fear and the age of the boy
The aftermath silence — not careful distance but the quiet of two people who have been inside the same pain

payoffs_to_land.description: The resonance that began as faint proximity-awareness in Ch5 now transmits full sensory memory — the bond's capacity has deepened

prev_tail_excerpt:
...acts she had built her professional life around.

But she was lying in a house that breathed, in a bed the house had warmed for her, feeling the insomnia of a man two floors away in her own chest, and the clinical vocabulary she'd built to contain this was not holding.

The anchor points. The filum. Tiberius Malfoy's six-hundred-year-old maintenance records and the damaged infrastructure that was killing ambient magic across Britain. She'd spent the afternoon and evening building the argument — the research case, the one she would present to the Department of Mysteries when she could get the data out of this library into a form the Ministry would accept. It was solid work. Important work. The kind of work she was built for, that justified the hours and the proximity and the fact that she was sleeping in a house that a woman in grey gloves had warned her not to trust.

But the resonance didn't care about the research. It cared about the body two floors above her, the Mark's dark magic restless and aching, the particular texture of his wakefulness at — she checked the clock on the nightstand — half past one in the morning. She'd started timing it. She knew that his worst nights ran past three. She knew that the Mark usually settled around four, as if the cursed magic exhausted itself and went dormant with the approach of dawn. She knew these things and she had told no one, and the knowing was becoming its own kind of intimacy, unwanted and ineradicable.

The house settled. The resonance hummed. Above her, Draco stopped pacing and was still.

She did not sleep for a long time.
state_deltas_required: The Mark floods shared memory during an examination — Draco relives taking the Mark at sixteen, and Hermione feels it through the resonance
She has seen his worst moment through his own sensory experience. The clinical frame between examiner and subject is permanently broken
